title: Utpaladeva: Īśvarasiddhi
author: Utpaladeva
summary: The Īśvarasiddhi ('Proof of the Lord') is one of the three short demonstrative treatises (Siddhitrayī) of Utpaladeva, the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') philosopher of nondual Kashmir Shaivism. It argues for the existence of Īśvara (Śiva) as the supreme conscious Subject (the highest 'I'), the ground of all cognition and being. Together with the Ajaḍapramātṛsiddhi and Sambandhasiddhi it supplements his major work, the Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā.
history: Utpaladeva was a pupil of Somānanda and the teacher of the lineage that culminated in Abhinavagupta, who built the Pratyabhijñā system into the philosophical core of Trika Shaivism. The Siddhitrayī survives with Utpaladeva's own auto-commentary (vṛtti); his works were later somewhat overshadowed by Abhinavagupta's more elaborate exegesis.

title: Matsyendranātha: Jñānakārikā
author: attributed to Matsyendranātha (school of Matsyendranātha)
date_estimate: c. 9th-11th c. CE (disputed; transmitted within the early Kaula corpus)
summary: The Jñānakārikā is a short Kaula doctrinal text in verse belonging to the tradition associated with Matsyendranātha, founder of the Yoginī Kaula school. It treats esoteric Kaula gnosis (jñāna): the nature of kula, the union of Śiva and Śakti, and the inner practice leading to liberation. It is one of the minor texts grouped with the Kaulajñānanirṇaya.
history: It was edited and published by P. C. Bagchi alongside the Kaulajñānanirṇaya as part of the 'minor texts of the school of Matsyendranātha.' Attribution to the semi-legendary Matsyendranātha is traditional rather than securely historical; the texts reflect an early-medieval Kaula milieu later honored by Abhinavagupta.

title: Puṇyānanda: Kāmakalāvilāsa
author: Puṇyānanda (Puṇyānandanātha)
summary: The Kāmakalāvilāsa ('Play of the Kāmakalā') is a concise Śrīvidyā work expounding the cosmogonic emanation of the universe from the supreme Goddess through the kāmakalā—the union of consciousness (prakāśa) and reflective awareness (vimarśa), bindu, nāda, and the unfolding of the Śrīcakra. It is a foundational metaphysical text for the worship of Tripurasundarī.
history: Puṇyānanda was an adherent of the Hādimata of Śrīvidyā and is credited with a commentary on the Yoginīhṛdaya. The Kāmakalāvilāsa is traditionally read with the Cidvallī commentary of Naṭanānanda(nātha); it was edited and translated into English by Arthur Avalon (Sir John Woodroffe) in the Tantric Texts series.

title: Kaṅkālamālinītantra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: medieval (c. 11th-16th c. CE; precise date uncertain)
summary: The Kaṅkālamālinītantra ('Tantra of Her who is Garlanded with Skeletons/Bones') is a short Śākta tantra of five paṭalas focused on the worship of the goddess Kālī. Cast as a dialogue between Śiva and the Goddess, it gives mantras, ritual visualizations, mythic narratives, and allegorical teaching on consciousness and liberation, belonging to the Kālīkula (Kālī-centered) current.
history: Its colophons ascribe it to the Dakṣiṇāmnāya (Southern transmission). It survives in printed Sanskrit and Bengali editions and is associated with the eastern Indian (Bengal) tantric milieu; like many such tantras it is anonymous and of uncertain date.

title: Matsyendranātha: Kaulajñānanirṇaya
author: attributed to Matsyendranātha (Matsyendrapāda)
date_estimate: c. 9th-11th c. CE (manuscript assigned to 9th-11th c.; tradition dates it earlier)
summary: The Kaulajñānanirṇaya ('Ascertainment of Kaula Gnosis') is the foundational scripture of the Yoginī Kaula school attributed to Matsyendranātha. In some 24 sections (paṭalas) it expounds the meaning of kula, Śiva-Śakti nonduality, bindu and nāda, modes of worship, the acquisition of yogic powers (siddhis), and esoteric—often erotic and 'left-handed'—practices for liberation, written in deliberately veiled 'twilight language.'
history: Edited from a Nepalese manuscript by P. C. Bagchi (1934), it is a key witness to early Kaula Tantra. Although its author already knew earlier Kaula scriptures (the 'Kulāgama'), Matsyendranātha is celebrated as a founder of Kaulism; Abhinavagupta honored him as a patriarch of yoga. It is central to the Nātha and Yoginī Kaula traditions.

title: Kaulopaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional
tradition: Hindu (Śākta/Kaula; classed as a Śākta Upaniṣad)
summary: The Kaulopaniṣad (Kaula Upaniṣad) is a brief esoteric text affiliated with the Atharvaveda (Saubhāgyakāṇḍa) that expounds Kaula and Śākta-Vedānta doctrine in aphoristic form. It emphasizes inner spiritual realization over external ritual and is intended only for the qualified (adhikārin), treating the nondual identity of the Self with the supreme reality.
history: Considered controversial for its Kaula leanings, it was sometimes excluded from Upaniṣadic collections. It is preserved with the Sanskrit commentary of Bhāskararāya (Bhāskara Rāi, 18th c.) and was published by Arthur Avalon in the Tantric Texts series (Kaula and Other Upanishads, 1922).

title: Kramadīpikā
author: Keśava Kāśmīrī Bhaṭṭa (text attributed to him; 'Rasikamohana Caṭṭopādhyāya' is the modern editor, not the author)
date_estimate: text c. 15th-16th c. CE (Keśava Kāśmīrī fl. late 15th-16th c.)
summary: The Kramadīpikā ('Lamp of the Sequence') is a Vaiṣṇava tantric/āgamic ritual manual centered on the worship of Kṛṣṇa-Gopāla and Rādhā-Govinda. Arranged in paṭalas, it sets out mantras (including the Gopāla and Vāsudeva 'mṛtyu' mantras), the sequence of ritual installation and worship, and instructions for devotees.
history: It is traditionally ascribed to Keśava Kāśmīrī Bhaṭṭa of the Nimbārka Sampradāya and was influential in the ritual of the Jagannātha temple at Purī and quoted in the Gauḍīya Haribhaktivilāsa. The name 'Rasikamohana Caṭṭopādhyāya' in the title hint refers to Rasik Mohan Chatterji, the 19th-century editor/publisher associated with printed Tantric texts, not the original author.

title: Kubjikātantra
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: Kubjikātantra denotes the scriptural corpus of the cult of the goddess Kubjikā ('the crooked/hunchbacked one'), the supreme Śakti and personification of kuṇḍalinī in the Paścimāmnāya (Western Transmission) of Kaula Shaivism. The root scripture is the Kubjikāmatatantra, which expounds the goddess, the deity Navātman, the maṇḍala, mantras, and yogic-ritual practice. Kubjikā is visualized as six-faced and twelve-armed, garlanded with skulls and serpents.
history: The Western Transmission is one of the four 'āmnāya' currents of Kaula Shaivism; the earliest evidence for the Kubjikā cult is from the tenth century, and its scriptures were shaped by both the Trika and Krama systems. The tradition flourished especially in Nepal, where its manuscripts are best preserved.

title: Kulārṇavatantra
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: The Kulārṇavatantra ('Ocean of the Kula') is a leading scripture of the Kaula school of Śāktism, comprising some seventeen chapters and around 2,000 verses cast as a dialogue between Śiva and the Goddess. It expounds Kaula worship—including the five 'M's (pañcamakāra)—the path to Self-realization through detachment, and devotes notable chapters to the guru-disciple (guru-śiṣya) relationship, mantra, and ritual discipline.
history: Widely regarded as one of the most authoritative and frequently quoted Kaula tantras, it is extensively cited in later tantric literature and digests. Arthur Avalon (Woodroffe) published an influential edition and English readings of the text.

title: Lalleśvarī: Lalleśvarīvākyāni
author: Lalleśvarī (Lal Dĕd / Lalla)
tradition: Hindu (Kashmir Shaivism; mystic devotional, with Sufi resonances)
summary: The Lalleśvarīvākyāni ('Sayings of Lalleśvarī') are the terse mystical verses (vākhs) of the 14th-century Kashmiri yoginī Lal Dĕd, rooted in nondual Kashmir Shaivism. They express the inward quest for the Self/Śiva, the futility of empty ritual and ascetic extremism, the control of breath and mind, and direct experiential realization, in a vivid vernacular voice.
history: Composed in early Kashmiri (with Sanskritic and emerging Persian elements), the vākhs are among the earliest attested literary works in the Kashmiri language. Transmitted orally for centuries, they were first systematically recorded in writing in the early 20th century (George Grierson's Lallā-Vākyāni, 1920). Lal Dĕd is revered by both Hindus and Muslims in Kashmir.

title: Śitikaṇṭha: Mahānayaprakāśa
author: Śitikaṇṭha
summary: The Mahānayaprakāśa ('Illumination of the Great Way') of Śitikaṇṭha is a Krama-tradition work of about ninety-four verses in Apabhraṃśa ('Old Kashmiri') accompanied by a Sanskrit commentary. It describes the inner structure of consciousness in relation to body and world and the esoteric, antinomian ('left-handed') practice of the Krama, devoted to the fierce goddesses led by Kālī.
history: It is one of three distinct Kashmiri Krama texts bearing the title Mahānayaprakāśa (others by Arṇasiṃha and an anonymous author). Notable for its language ideology and Old-Kashmiri verse, it remains an understudied but important witness to the radical nondualist Krama school.

title: Mahānayaprakāśa (anonymous)
author: anonymous (one of several Krama works of this title; sometimes ascribed to Arṇasiṃha)
summary: This is the Sanskrit Mahānayaprakāśa ('Illumination of the Great Way'), an anonymous Krama treatise distinct from Śitikaṇṭha's Old-Kashmiri work of the same name. It systematically expounds the Krama doctrine of the sequential (krama) emanation and reabsorption of reality through the cycle of goddesses, the nature of nondual consciousness, and the esoteric soteriology of the Kālī-centered school.
history: There are at least three Kashmiri Krama texts titled Mahānayaprakāśa—one by Śitikaṇṭha, one by a disciple named Arṇasiṃha, and one anonymous. The 'unknown author' designation here reflects this ambiguity in the catalogue record; all belong to the Krama lineage of Kashmir Shaivism.

title: Maheśvarānanda: Mahārthamañjarī
author: Maheśvarānanda
tradition: Hindu (Kashmir Shaivism, Krama / Mahārtha; composed in South India)
summary: The Mahārthamañjarī ('Bouquet of the Great Truth') is a compact work of about seventy verses (originally in Mahārāṣṭrī Prakrit with the author's own Sanskrit rendering) expounding the 'Great Truth' (mahārtha) of the Krama/Kālīkrama tradition. With the author's Parimala auto-commentary, it presents Krama metaphysics integrated with Pratyabhijñā recognition philosophy.
history: Maheśvarānanda, working in South India, was an exponent of the Kālīkrama who quotes earlier Krama authorities (e.g., the Kramakeli) and thereby transmits the Kashmiri Krama into the South. The text is an important late synthesis of Krama and Pratyabhijñā thought.

title: Makuṭāgama
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: early-medieval (1st millennium CE; precise date uncertain)
summary: The Makuṭāgama (Makuṭa Āgama) is one of the twenty-eight Siddhānta Śaivāgamas, scriptures regarded as revealed by Śiva that prescribe temple construction, consecration, ritual, and worship. It emphasizes the 'crown/regal' (makuṭa) aspect of Śiva—his sovereign authority—and is the āgama traditionally followed in the daily worship of the Naṭarāja temple at Chidambaram.
history: Counted among the canonical Siddhānta āgamas of South Indian Śaiva Siddhānta, it survives mainly in manuscript and is used liturgically in temple practice. Like other āgamas it is anonymous and of uncertain date, transmitted through priestly lineages.

title: Mṛgendrāgama
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 6th-9th c. CE (the influential Vidyāpāda relatively early)
summary: The Mṛgendrāgama (Mṛgendratantra) is a Śaiva Siddhānta scripture, treated as an upāgama / supplement of the Kāmikāgama. Its four pādas cover doctrine (vidyā), yoga, ritual (kriyā), and conduct (caryā); the Vidyāpāda in particular is a key statement of Siddhānta metaphysics—the categories of pati (Lord), paśu (bound soul), and pāśa (bonds).
history: It is one of the four āgamas relied upon by Śaiva Siddhānta that fall outside the standard list of twenty-eight. The tenth-century Kashmiri exegete Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha wrote an authoritative commentary (vṛtti) on it, and it was quoted and emulated by twelfth-century South Indian Siddhānta authors such as Aghoraśiva.

title: Netratantra
author: anonymous/traditional
tradition: Hindu (Śaiva, Mantramārga; non-Saiddhāntika, later read in Kashmiri nondualism)
date_estimate: c. 700-850 CE (composed in or before the 9th c., Kashmir)
summary: The Netratantra ('Tantra of the Eye'), also called Mṛtyujit and Amṛteśavidhāna, centers on the cult of Amṛteśvara(bhairava) ('Lord of Ambrosia') and Amṛtalakṣmī. As a dialogue in which Bhairava instructs the Goddess, it teaches metaphysics, cosmology, and soteriology together with apotropaic ritual, yoga, and the netra-mantra (Mṛtyuñjaya) for conquering death and warding off ills—addressing a deliberately broad range of deities and aims.
history: A non-Saiddhāntika Mantramārga scripture, it was provided with an influential commentary (Netroddyota) by the 11th-century Kashmiri Pratyabhijñā master Kṣemarāja, who read it through a nondualist lens; it is an important source for early Śaiva ritual and royal religion.

title: Sadyojyoti: Nareśvaraparīkṣā
author: Sadyojyoti(s)
date_estimate: c. late 7th-early 8th c. CE (Sadyojyoti fl. c. 675-725 CE)
summary: The Nareśvaraparīkṣā ('Examination of [the relation of] Man and the Lord') is a foundational philosophical treatise of dualist Śaiva Siddhānta by Sadyojyoti, the earliest systematizer of Siddhānta theology in Sanskrit. It investigates the natures of the bound soul (nara/paśu) and the Lord (īśvara/pati) and their relation, defending the Siddhānta categories of Lord, soul, and bonds against rival positions.
history: Sadyojyoti grounded his exposition in scriptures such as the Rauravāgama and Svāyambhuvasūtrasaṅgraha. The Nareśvaraparīkṣā received an important commentary (prakāśa) by the Kashmiri theologian Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha, and the work shaped the later, highly systematic Saiddhāntika exegesis of tenth-century Kashmir and twelfth-century South India.

title: Vedāntadeśika: Pāñcarātrarakṣā
author: Vedānta Deśika (Veṅkaṭanātha)
summary: The Pāñcarātrarakṣā ('Defense of the Pāñcarātra') is Vedānta Deśika's apologetic treatise vindicating the scriptural authority (prāmāṇya) and validity of the Pāñcarātra Āgama against critics. It argues for the self-validity of the Pāñcarātra Saṃhitās and the universality of Pāñcarātra ritual and ethics, defending Vaiṣṇava temple and domestic worship within the Śrī Vaiṣṇava tradition.
history: Vedānta Deśika (Veṅkaṭanātha), the great Śrī Vaiṣṇava ācārya, builds on Yāmunācārya's earlier Āgamaprāmāṇya in defending Pāñcarātra orthodoxy. The work is one of his several 'rakṣā' (defense) treatises and remains authoritative in Śrī Vaiṣṇava liturgical and theological tradition.

title: Abhinavagupta: Paramārthasāra
author: Abhinavagupta
summary: The Paramārthasāra ('Essence of the Supreme Truth') is Abhinavagupta's concise compendium of nondual Trika Shaivism in 105 verses. It presents the metaphysics of Śiva as universal consciousness, the soul's bondage and liberation, the emanation of the tattvas, and the path of recognition (pratyabhijñā) and grace leading to identity with Śiva.
history: Abhinavagupta refashioned an earlier non-Śaiva Paramārthasāra (the Ādhārakārikā attributed to Ādiśeṣa / Śeṣamuni) into a Trika scripture. The work is a popular, accessible summary of the system elaborated at length in his Tantrāloka and Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī, and was commented upon (e.g., by Yogarāja).

title: Parātrīśikālaghuvṛtti
author: Abhinavagupta (the catalogue 'unknown' author hint is incorrect)
summary: The Parātrīśikālaghuvṛtti (also Parātriṃśikālaghuvṛtti, alias Anuttaratattvavimarśinī) is Abhinavagupta's short commentary on the Parātrīśikā (Parātriṃśikā), the c. 36-verse Trika tantra esteemed as a summit of Kashmir Shaivism. It expounds the Supreme Reality (anuttara/parā) and the theory and practice of mantra and phonematic emanation as the means of realization.
history: This is the concise of Abhinavagupta's two commentaries on the Parātrīśikā; the other is the much longer Parātrīśikāvivaraṇa, and the two differ in interpretation as well as length. The text is a core source for Trika mantra-theory and was edited within the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies.
